title: ‘Babylon,’ ‘White Noise,’ and 10 Oscars Contenders to Stream Now
format: The last show of 2022. Sean and Amanda tell listeners where to stream two dozen awards contenders, then review Damien Chazelle's 'Babylon' and Noah Baumbach's 'White Noise.'

segments.heading: Where to Stream the 2022 Oscar Contenders

films.title: Elvis
films.note: On HBO Max. Baz Luhrmann up to his old tricks, moving fast through Presley's life.

films.title: The Banshees of Inisherin
films.note: Also on HBO Max. Didn't do much in theaters but they expect its profile to rise at home, the way these movies do once they hit the streaming carousel.

films.title: The Woman King
films.note: Gina Prince-Bythewood's film, on VOD; a Sony release headed to Netflix eventually. They compare it to Braveheart and The Last of the Mohicans and think it could surge once it lands on a home screen, maybe pushing Viola Davis into the race.

films.title: The Invitation
films.note: Horror release cited as a timing benchmark; expected on streaming around Christmas, which sets a rough January window for The Woman King.

films.title: The Fabelmans
films.note: On VOD. Spielberg's adolescence movie; Sean no longer thinks the Best Picture juice is coming for it. Two hours 40, which Amanda says you should still watch in one sitting.

films.title: Aftersun
films.note: The A24 drama with Paul Mescal and Frankie Corio, recently streaming. Adam Nayman writes about it well on their Best of 2022 list.

films.title: Black Panther: Wakanda Forever
films.note: The one release they can't place; probably Disney+ and VOD simultaneously. They don't think it's competing for an Oscar.

films.title: Triangle of Sadness
films.note: Ruben Ostlund's Palme d'Or winner, on VOD and picking up steam; a Neon film, so Hulu soon. They read its appeal as international voters enjoying a laugh at American elites.

films.title: She Said
films.note: On VOD, Peacock soon. Feels abandoned in the race.

films.title: Tár
films.note: On VOD, Peacock soon. Both hosts' No. 2 movie of the year; Amanda says it plays really well at home.

films.title: Armageddon Time
films.note: James Gray's film, on VOD and likely Peacock soon since it's a Focus release. They admire it but still haven't discussed it in depth.

films.title: Nope
films.note: On Peacock. Sean admits he didn't connect with it; both wonder whether it's too late for a Best Picture push despite a real critical groundswell and an AFI list nod.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: On Paramount+. Sean thinks it's secretly in the driver's seat for Best Picture.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Arrives on Paramount+ December 30, seven months after its Memorial Day opening. They're nervous Avatar takes its blockbuster slot but think both get nominated.

films.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
films.note: On Netflix; floated as a good family watch.

films.title: Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
films.note: Alejandro Gonzalez Inarritu's autobiographical swing, on Netflix; discussed at length the previous week with Adam Nayman.

films.title: RRR
films.note: On Netflix, trying to make a late awards bid, which they'd find really fun.

films.title: All Quiet on the Western Front
films.note: On Netflix; they defer a full discussion to a later international-feature segment.

films.title: Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio
films.note: On Netflix.

films.title: Emancipation
films.note: The Will Smith vehicle, on Apple TV+.

films.title: Decision to Leave
films.note: Park Chan-wook's film, on MUBI and rentable on VOD.

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: Still in theaters, and the source of Amanda's Oscar-season anxiety; the hosts were mixed on it.

films.title: Living
films.note: In theaters, just opened. Oliver Hermanus's version of Kurosawa's Ikiru, adapted by Kazuo Ishiguro.

films.title: The Whale
films.note: In theaters. Not a film Sean likes.

films.title: EO
films.note: Jerzy Skolimowski's portrait of a donkey, in theaters and an Amanda favorite; one of several 2022 movies about animals.

films.title: Empire of Light
films.note: Sam Mendes's misfire, headed to HBO Max soon. Sean called it ham-fisted out of Telluride but won't rule out the Academy, given Mendes's track record through 1917.

films.title: Babylon
films.note: Damien Chazelle's three-hour-plus chronicle of Hollywood's silent-to-sound transition, built around four composites: Margot Robbie's Nellie LaRoy (modeled on Clara Bow), Brad Pitt's Jack Conrad (John Gilbert with dashes of Gable and Fairbanks), Diego Calva's Manny Torres, and Jovan Adepo's jazz-musician Sidney Palmer, plus Jean Smart as an Eleanor Glyn-type columnist and Li Jun Li as an Anna May Wong figure. Sean says it makes him feel alive and loves the swing. Amanda finds the excess irritating even where it's the point and calls the cocaine spectacle boring; she pegs Pitt as miscast for the first two acts before he lands the last third. Both point to the making-of sequences and the sound-stage scene as the film's high point. They read the Singin' in the Rain-drenched finale as Chazelle's semi-abstract statement on cinema and hear in it a spiteful reckoning with his own La La Land. Guest Bobby Wagner loved it.

films.title: White Noise
films.note: Noah Baumbach's adaptation of Don DeLillo's 1985 novel, Sean's sometime favorite book. Adam Driver plays Hitler-studies professor Jack Gladney and Greta Gerwig his wife Babette; a train-and-tanker collision sets off the Airborne Toxic Event. They praise the campus-satire first two-thirds and the literalized Spielbergian disaster set piece, and call the Hitler-vs.-Elvis lecture the moment Driver could win an Oscar. But they say the surreal final act doesn't translate: DeLillo's atmosphere is what the plot strips away. Sean calls it a soft recommend that goes out on a high with the LCD Soundsystem supermarket-dance closer, a costly, unrepeatable Netflix swing they liken to Bardo.
